card.title: How a new Florida congressional map could cost Democrats seats
card.description: The redrawn congressional district lines that Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis will soon sign into law are intended to help Republicans pick up as many as four U.S. House seats in November — a scenario that would cost some Democrats their seats. The GOP-drawn map uses two redistricting tools called packing and cracking. In Tampa, officials cracked up a Democratic-held district across multiple districts. In Orlando, they packed Democratic voters from two districts into one. In south Florida, they redrew several seats to reduce five contiguous Democratic seats into a map that could limit Democrats to no more than three seats over the same extended area across Palm Beach, Broward and Miami-Dade counties.

card.title: ​When a child stops sharing things with parents, it usually begins with these 6 parental habits
card.description: Children rarely stop sharing with parents in one dramatic moment. More often, the distance grows quietly, shaped by small, repeated interactions that make honesty feel unsafe, unnecessary or simply not worth the effort. A child who once narrated every detail of the day may slowly begin to realise it feels safer to hide feelings, skim over problems or answer in one-word replies.This is not always rebellion. Sometimes it is self-protection. When children feel dismissed, judged or constantly managed, they learn to keep parts of themselves private. These six everyday habits often sit at the root of that change.

card.description: Unitree has extended its R1 dual‑arm humanoid robot family with new R1-A5 and R1-A7 models, which can be fitted with 2-finger grippers or 3 or 5-finger dexterous hands, and attached to a fixed base or a wheeled base for indoor mobility. The new robots appear based on the low-cost Unitree R1 platform launched last year, which can dance, walk, run, perform kung-fu moves, and chat with users, but is otherwise not overly useful since it lacks dexterous hands. The R1-A5 and R1-A7 won't be able to dance, since they don't come with legs, but the upper body comes with a head and two arms equipped with grippers or dexterous hands, which could perform useful tasks in combination with binocular vision. content:
For a comparison between Windows 95/98 and NT 4.0: I picked up a cheap 3Com PCI Ethernet card for $10 and put it into my 1996-1997 era desktop. Windows 98 SE promptly began hanging during boot when an Ethernet cable wasn't plugged in due to the drivers locking up boot waiting for a DHCP server until I installed unofficial patches, and with or without those I still can't get it to do anything useful including a ping.

Windows NT 4.0 got onto the network first try without any manual address configuration or hangs. There are advantages to being a real operating system with a non-toy network stack. :p

content:
English – The Conversation | Making tech giants pay for news was a success the first time around. It can be done again by Rod Sims, Enterprise Professor, Melbourne Institute of Applied Economic and Social Research, The University of Melbourne

AI generated summary, Read the full article for complete information.

The article reviews Australia’s pioneering News Media Bargaining Code, which forced Google and Meta to negotiate payments with news organisations after a market‑failure analysis showed that platforms profit from news content without compensating its producers. Introduced following the ACCC’s Digital Platform Inquiry, the code required designated platforms to negotiate with all eligible public‑interest media or face arbitration, leading to over AU$1 billion in deals—about AU$250 million per year—within the first five years, with smaller outlets often receiving higher per‑journalist payments. When Meta’s three‑year contracts expired it withdrew news, highlighting a weakness of the original scheme that only applied to platforms actually carrying news. To address this, the government is now proposing a News Bargaining Incentive that would apply to platforms regardless of news carriage, setting payment benchmarks and imposing a higher “charge” if deals are not made, though it removes the arbitration fallback and allows platforms to negotiate with as few as four outlets. The author argues that while the incentive introduces new complexities, it continues Australia’s leadership in protecting journalism and could be legislated by mid‑year, provided refinements are made.

Read more: https://theconversation.com/making-tech-giants-pay-for-news-was-a-success-the-first-time-around-it-can-be-done-again-281865

media_attachments.description: Speaker vent holes in the bottom of my so-called Sleepbox.  There are 36 round holes arranged in 3 concentric circles around a center hole.  The holes are only about an eighth of an inch in diameter while the whole array is about 1.5 inches in diameter in the bottom of a 3-inch-wide square unit.  The Sleepbox uses these vent holes to send any of 25 soothing sounds into the night air to lull me to sleep.  Well, when I have it turned on, anyway.  These sounds include white noise, fan noise, running brook, sea waves, singing birds, distant trains, and others.  I bought it back in January to help me sleep better.  Although I haven't actually used it, it's helped me sleep better anyway.  Just knowing that I now have the power to fill my bedroom at 4 a.m. with any of these sounds at the touch of a button is very reassuring somehow.  I may not be able to do anything about soaring gas prices or the Supreme Court, but I CAN generate the sound of THREE different types of rain without getting out of bed!  WOO-HOO!  (Please don't mistake me for a god.)
